a We evaluate UntrustVul on 155K vulnerability predictions by four

by up to 321% in F1-score and 100% in trustworthiness.

Machine learning (ML) has been widely used in vulnerability de-
tection to identify weaknesses in information systems, security
protocols, or implementations that can be exploited by a threat
source [24]. Recent studies [4, 6, 13, 65] have shown promising
results, with state-of-the-art (SOTA) models achieving up to 95%
accuracy in held-out evaluations for detecting vulnerable functions.
To review vulnerabilities detected by ML predictions and identify
root causes [52, 54], developers need to manually assess suspicious
lines highlighted by fine-grained interpretations [5, 13, 31, 62]. How-
ever, this raises a critical question: Should we rely on vulnerability
predictions of ML models?
Investigating their behaviors, studies [4, 5, 14, 51] have found
that ML models often learn spurious correlations with irrelevant
code parts during training, such as identifiers, coding styles, or
comments that frequently appear in vulnerable code. Hence, these
models can correctly detect vulnerable functions without relying
on the actual vulnerabilities. As a result, they can provide mislead-
ing interpretations, hindering developers from locating the root
causes and increasing the burden of manual assessment. Worse,
following these interpretations risks creating flawed patches that
fail to address existing vulnerabilities, or even introduce new ones.
This paper considers a vulnerability prediction untrustworthy if
it highlights lines unrelated to actual vulnerabilities. Untrustworthy
predictions can lead developers to easily overlook the actual root
causes, resulting in severe threats [46]. For example, the function
vrrp_print_data shown in Figure 1 contains a CWE-59 vulnerabil-
ity at Line 7, accessing a file without preventing it from resolving

This section clarifies the definition of trustworthiness and the mo-
tivation for detecting untrustworthy vulnerability predictions.
2.1  Trustworthiness in Vulnerability Detection
Trustworthiness is a complex concept, making it challenging for
researchers to reach a consensus on a unified definition [25, 29, 55].
Although it is not easy to choose one specific definition, we consider
trustworthiness as a system’s ability to (a) work properly in a
given context and (b) justify a stakeholder’s belief, as defined by
Kästner et al. [26]. Fulfilling condition (a) primarily depends on the
system, whereas condition (b) involves the stakeholder. We refine
the definition for vulnerability detection. For (a), a vulnerability
detection model, which classifies a function as either vulnerable or
non-vulnerable, should correctly predict vulnerable functions. For
(b), with developers as the stakeholders, such predictions should
annotate lines of code related to the actual vulnerabilities [54].
We refer to vulnerability predictions, or shortly predictions, as
classifications that label code as vulnerable and annotate lines of
code that they deem suspicious. Accordingly, we define an untrust-
worthy vulnerability prediction as follows.
Definition 1. An untrustworthy prediction correctly flags a vul-
nerable function but highlights vulnerability-unrelated lines.
Following Definition 1, a vulnerability prediction that highlights
irrelevant lines, e.g., comments and delimiters, is untrustworthy. In
this paper, we focus on correct vulnerability predictions only, as
incorrect ones can annotate meaningless code parts.
Existing studies [34] have acknowledged the use of ML expla-
nations for trustworthiness assessment by disclosing the decision-
making process. These explanations are categorised as global [3],
which provide insights into the entire model’s workings, and lo-
cal [30, 44, 61], which focus on individual predictions. Local ex-
planations, which break down a model into its components, allow
users to grasp its decision-making process in a way that aligns
with human cognitive patterns. Hence, prior studies [13, 61] have
leveraged them for fine-grained interpretations of vulnerability
predictions at the line level. Such interpretations, or explanations,
take the form of a list of suspicious lines of code, each paired with
an importance score quantifying its contribution to the predictions.

Predictions  Predictions  are misleading
② Manual Assessment  ② Identifying Untrustworthiness ③ Manual Assessment
(a) Traditional  (b) With UntrustVul
Figure 3: Two processes of ML-based vulnerability detection.
important not to incidentally exclude any trustworthy predictions,
leaving the vulnerabilities unattended.
UntrustVul is essential for exposing untrustworthy vul-
nerability predictions during inference, as prediction certainty
alone is insufficient. Figure 3b illustrates the vulnerability detection
process integrated with UntrustVul. To ensure UntrustVul’s
helpfulness, we design it to integrate seamlessly into developers’
workflow. UntrustVul acts as a filter between vulnerability pre-
dictions and developers, exposing untrustworthy predictions. By
doing so, UntrustVul consider the same inputs as developers in
the traditional process, which are individual vulnerability predic-
tions that annotate suspicious lines of code. Developers can now
recognise untrustworthy predictions, decreasing the willingness
to rely on them. Conversely, they can also identify trustworthy
predictions, reducing the efforts of manual assessment.
3  UNTRUSTVUL
UntrustVul is an automated approach that can detect untrustwor-
thy vulnerability predictions. It considers as inputs a vulnerability
prediction during inference and annotated suspicious lines of code.
The key idea is to assess the vulnerability-relatedness of these sus-
picious lines. The prediction is deemed untrustworthy if they are
mainly vulnerability-unrelated. A line is deemed as vulnerability-
unrelated if it ① cannot trigger vulnerabilities, and ② does not serve
as a pivotal point in the flow reaching vulnerabilities.
We assess ① by determining whether the line differs from his-
torical vulnerabilities. Specifically, we encode the single line into
a deep representation using transformer-based code models. This
deep representation is then compared with the deep representations
of historical vulnerable lines. We frame this comparison as a binary
classification task and apply a supervised learning-based approach
to determine whether a line resembles historical non-vulnerable
or vulnerable lines. Lines classified as resembling historical non-
vulnerable lines are called benign candidates. We estimate ② by
evaluating whether there is a sequence of dependencies from the
line to one that is not a benign candidate, in particular, control
dependencies or data dependencies that manipulate a variable in-
volved in the target line. The longer the sequence, the less related
the two lines are. We refer to the length of this sequence as the
vulnerability-reachability distance, which quantifies the relat-
edness between two lines. Finally, UntrustVul calculates a score,
denoted by T , by summarising the ratio between each suspicious
line’s importance score and its vulnerability-reachability distance.

A lower T indicates the prediction is more likely untrustworthy. Fig-
ure 4 shows the process of UntrustVul, which consists of three
steps: code parsing, line-level assessment, and dependency-level
assessment. The last two steps assess ① and ②, respectively.
3.1  Source Code Parsing
UntrustVul takes as input a vulnerability prediction and its cor-
responding source code. The prediction also annotates a list of
suspicious lines and their importance scores that quantify their
contributions. This can be represented as 𝐸 = {⟨𝑙𝑖 , 𝑠𝑖 ⟩}, where 𝑙𝑖 is
a line of code, and 𝑠𝑖 indicates its importance score.
For static code analysis in dependency-level assessment, Un-
trustVul parses the source code into a program dependence graph
(PDG) [12]. PDGs are directed graphs explicitly representing depen-
dencies among statements and predicate expressions. In particular,
they are constructed using two types of edges: (1) data dependency
edges reflecting the influence of one variable on another, and (2)
control dependency edges corresponding to the control flow be-
tween the statements. UntrustVul leverages Joern [2], a platform
for source code analysis widely used in existing studies [4, 31, 65],
to parse the source code into a PDG. We simplify the generated
PDG by merging nodes from the same line into a single node, re-
taining their dependencies. The simplified PDG contains nodes
representing lines and the dependencies between them.
UntrustVul then combines the simplified PDG and the list 𝐸
into a weighted PDG, denoted by 𝐺, by assigning each node with
the importance score of the corresponding line. For example, the
prediction shown in Figure 1 highlights suspicious lines and their
importance scores, as described on the right side of the figure. The
simplified PDG of vrrp_print_data is represented in Figure 6, with
each node corresponding to a line of code.

We attach the importance
score for each node as follows: 0.13 to Node 1, 0.06 to Node 3, 0.18
to Node 4, 0.27 to Node 5, 0.08 to Node 7, 0.19 to Node 8, and 0.04 to
Node 9. Other importance scores are ignored, as the corresponding
lines are not represented in the PDG.
3.2  Line-level Assessment for Identifying
Benign Candidates
This step aims to identify lines that, based on their syntax, are
unlikely to trigger vulnerabilities on their own, called benign can-
didates. We apply a supervised learning-based approach to classify
a line as either a benign candidate or not. Specifically, we train clas-
sifiers using historical vulnerable lines and non-vulnerable lines as
training data.
Worth mentioning, line-level assessment differs from the origi-
nal vulnerability detection task, which considers each function as

a whole. Models for the original task must comprehend all lines
within their contexts. In contrast, line-level assessment is more tar-
geted, examining each line independently, while its broader context
is assessed in dependency-level assessment. Classifiers trained for
line-level assessment only learn patterns at the syntax level.
We extract historical vulnerable and non-vulnerable lines by
leveraging BigVul dataset, following the process detailed in Sec-
tion 3.2.1. To improve the effectiveness of line-level assessment, we
employ ensemble learning [28] by aggregating multiple classifiers,
which is described in Section 3.2.2.
3.2.1  Dataset construction. The process of dataset construc-
tion for training classifiers in line-level assessment is described
in Figure 5. We leverage BigVul dataset [10], which provides non-
vulnerable and vulnerable functions with detailed line-level fixes
within git commits that reflect the changes addressing vulnerabil-
ities. Following prior research [7, 10, 19, 20, 31], we adopt lines
that are deleted or modified in the commit as historical vulnerable
lines, excluding redundant lines such as comments, blank lines,
and delimiters. We then randomly select lines in non-vulnerable

between a line to references being the historical vulnerable lines.
As suggested by Wieting et al. [59], we choose 0.5 as the thresh-
𝐿−
old of BLEU scores. Only lines in  0 with a BLEU score below 0.5
are considered historical non-vulnerable lines. After collecting the
data, a dataset of historical vulnerable and non-vulnerable lines is
constructed. To minimise the risk of spurious correlations learnt
during training, all lines are normalised by refactoring them into
a consistent coding convention. The dataset is then used to train
classifiers to identify benign candidates.
3.2.2  Ensemble learning. We leverage recent SOTA transformer-
based architectures, such as CodeBERT [11], GraphCodeBERT [18],
and UniXcoder [17], to encode lines into vector representations.
These vector representations are then used to train binary classifiers
that identify benign candidates based on their syntaxes. These
SOTA models, however, can be biased due to their training data,
potentially affecting the ability of line-level assessment. To mitigate
this, UntrustVul applies ensemble learning [28] by aggregating 𝐾
classifiers, 𝑀ens = {𝑀1, 𝑀2, ..., 𝑀𝐾 }. Each classifier 𝑀𝑖 outputs 1 if
it labels a line as a benign candidate, and 0 otherwise. The ensemble
model 𝑀ens detects benign candidates by combining the decisions

3.3  Dependency-level Assessment for
Measuring Vulnerability Reachability
Dependency-level assessment verifies whether benign candidates
are related to other suspicious lines by reaching through dependen-
cies. Specifically, UntrustVul assesses their dependencies via two
criteria: vulnerable dependencies and vulnerability reachability.
Vulnerable dependencies. Dependencies between suspicious
lines annotated by a prediction should be meaningful and lead
to vulnerabilities. Intuitively, trustworthy models should rely on
control and data dependencies, which manipulate the code flow and
variable changes that can trigger vulnerabilities. We call them vul-
nerable dependencies, which indicate the dependencies with a strong
connection to the vulnerabilities. For example, Figure 6 shows the
PDG of the function vrrp_print_data presented in Figure 1. The
dependencies 1→3 and 3→7 all lead to the vulnerability at Line 7,
while the other dependencies, such as 3 → 4, 5 → 4, 7 → 8, and
8 → 9, do not. If the model overrelies on the latter dependencies, it
is likely to make an untrustworthy prediction.
To determine whether a dependency 𝑑 = 𝑥 → 𝑦 in the PDG
denoted by 𝐺 is vulnerable, UntrustVul analyses 𝑑’s type with
its start and end points using the rules listed in Table 1. We denote
this rule-based analysis as a function 𝑝(𝑑,𝐺), which returns 1 if
𝑑 satisfies the rules and 0 otherwise. For example, in Figure 6,
given that Line 7 is not a benign candidate while other lines are,
𝑝(1 → 3,𝐺) = 𝑝(3 → 7,𝐺) = 1, as they manipulate data and the
false branch of the if statement at Line 3. In contrast, 𝑝(3→4,𝐺) =

Vulnerability reachability. Given suspicious lines annotated by
the prediction, some are identified as benign candidates in line-
level assessment by 𝑀ens. To assess vulnerability reachability, Un-
trustVul examines whether these benign candidates have vulnera-
ble control or data dependencies to other suspicious lines. We define
the vulnerability-reachability distance between a benign candidate
and one that is not, as detailed in Definition 2.
Definition 2. The vulnerability-reachability distance between
the benign candidate 𝑙1 and the line 𝑙𝑛 in the Program Dependence
Graph 𝐺, denoted as 𝑟 (𝑙1, 𝑙𝑛,𝐺), is the length of the shortest sequence
of vulnerable dependencies connecting them. This sequence takes
the form 𝑙1 → 𝑙2, 𝑙2 → 𝑙3,  ..., 𝑙3 → 𝑙𝑛 in 𝐺, where ∀𝑖  ∈ [1, 𝑛)  :
𝑝(𝑙𝑖 →𝑙𝑖+1,𝐺) = 1. If such a sequence does not exist, 𝑟 (𝑙1, 𝑙𝑛,𝐺) = ∞.
As an example of the vulnerability-reachability distances, we re-
visit the PDG of the function vrrp_print_data shown in Figure 6.
Considering the benign candidates at Lines 4–5 and the vulnerable
line, Line 7, there is no path connecting them since they belong
to two different branches. This means 𝑟 (4, 7,𝐺) = 𝑟 (5, 7,𝐺) = ∞.
Similarly, there is no dependency from Line 8 to Line 7, making
𝑟 (8, 7,𝐺) = ∞. In contrast, we have 𝑟 (3, 4,𝐺) = 1, as Line 3 has a
vulnerable control dependency leading to Line 7.
We define function ℎ(𝑙,𝐺) that indicates whether the benign
candidate 𝑙 is related to those that are not, as shown in Equation 2.

Trustworthiness score computation. UntrustVul finally calcu-
lates a score, denoted by T , to estimate the trustworthiness of the
vulnerability prediction. The T score is computed by measuring.

We study the following four research questions (RQs):
RQ1. How effective is UntrustVul in detecting untrustworthy
vulnerability predictions?
RQ2. How efficient is UntrustVul in detecting untrustworthy
vulnerability predictions?
RQ3. How do different design choices affect UntrustVul?
RQ4. Can UntrustVul improve the model’s trustworthiness?
Metrics. We frame the task of detecting untrustworthy vulnerability
predictions as a binary classification task, with “untrustworthy” as
the positive class and “trustworthy” as the negative class. We then
use the following metrics to measure UntrustVul’s effectiveness.
• Accuracy: the proportion of predictions correctly labeled as
𝑇𝑃+𝑇𝑁
untrustworthy or not, calculated as 𝑇𝑃+𝐹𝑃+𝑇𝑁+𝐹𝑁 .
• AUC: the area under the receiver operating characteristic
curve, assessing the ability to distinguish between trustwor-
thy and untrustworthy predictions.
• Precision: the proportion of true untrustworthy predictions
𝑇𝑃
among the detected ones, defined as 𝑇𝑃+𝐹𝑃 .
• Sensitivity (or Recall): the proportion of untrustworthy pre-
𝑇𝑃
dictions correctly detected, defined as 𝑇𝑃+𝐹𝑁 .
• F1-score: the harmonic mean of Precision and Sensitivity, cal-
2×Pre×Sen
culated as  Pre+Sen  .
• Specificity: the proportion of correctly detected trustworthy
𝑇𝑁
predictions, defined as 𝑇𝑁+𝐹𝑃 .
• G-mean: the geometric mean of Sensitivity and Specificity,
√︁
calculated as  Sen × Spec.
F1-score is the balanced metric for assessing the performance of
detecting untrustworthy predictions. In addition, we also present
AUC and G-mean as the balanced metrics for evaluating classifi-
cation performance on both classes. In addition, we measure the
efficiency of the approaches by their processing time in seconds.
To answer RQ4, we report the ability for vulnerability detection
of improved models using F1-score. We also estimate their trustwor-
thiness using the average 𝐼𝑜𝑈 between suspicious lines identified
by the models and ground-truth line-level vulnerabilities, and the
average T score calculated by UntrustVul.
Baselines. We compare UntrustVul with the following baselines:
Naive is a straightforward approach that detects untrustwor-
thy vulnerability predictions solely based on model certainty. Our
6

study uses model confidence as a certainty metric. Predictions with
confidence < 𝜃  are considered untrustworthy. The optimal
conf
value of 𝜃  is determined by maximising G-mean.
conf
PerturbVar, PerturbAPI, and PerturbJoint [43] discover spurious
features used in vulnerability detection models by perturbing the
code and observing output changes. PerturbVar renames variables
to frequently occurring names in the opposite class, PerturbAPI
injects frequent API calls from the opposite class, and PerturbJoint
combines both PerturbVar and PerturbAPI. A prediction is deemed
untrustworthy if the perturbations alter it.
Notably, ML models that provide line-level detections can serve
as baselines by considering predictions untrustworthy if their over-
lapwith ground-truth line-level vulnerabilities is minimal. However,
since these models act similarly to the models under test, and the
procedure of labeling trustworthiness corresponds to the genera-
tion of ground-truth trustworthiness labels for predictions. To avoid
confusion, we treat line-level vulnerability detectors and similar
models as models under test.
Models under test. Experiments are conducted with predictions
made by four SOTA vulnerability detectors, as shown in Table 4.
• LineVul [13] leverages CodeBERT [11] to capture long-term
dependencies within a long sequence and adopts the atten-
tion mechanism to locate the vulnerable lines.
• SVulD [40] adopts contrastive learning to train a UniXcoder [17]
model to distinguish semantic representations of functions
regardless of their lexical similarity.
• IVDetect [31] represents code as programdependency graphs
(PDGs) and employs graph convolution networks with fea-
ture attention. It also uses GNNExplainer [61] to provide
line-level interpretations.
• ReVeal [4] translates code into a graph embedding and trains
a representation learner on the extracted features.
We use the implementations provided by the authors to generate
vulnerability predictions. Suspicious lines are identified using the
same explanation methods, which is validated for achieving the
best performance in fine-grained detection [13, 31, 66]. Specifically,
we leverage the attention mechanism for transformer-based models
and GNNExplainer [61] for graph-based models. For graph-based
models, we also apply Joern to parse source code into graphs, as
outlined in the original papers.
Datasets. This study requires datasets with line-level vulnerability
annotations. Certain datasets like Devign [65] and Reveal [4] only la-
bel vulnerable functions, making them unsuitable. D2A dataset [64]
provides vulnerable traces but suffers from a high false positive rate,
with over two-thirds of samples mislabeled [8], and is therefore
excluded in this study.

Instead, we use BigVul [10], MegaVul [39]
and SARD [1] datasets, which provide line-level vulnerabilities, to
evaluate UntrustVul. While BigVul is used for intra-project eval-
uations, MegaVul and SARD are used for cross-project evaluations.
To establish the ground-truth trustworthiness label for a predic-
tion, we quantify the overlap between suspicious lines (𝐸) annotated
by the prediction and ground-truth vulnerable lines (𝐺). Follow-
ing Hu et al. [20], we measure the overlap using Intersection over

Union, defined as 𝐼𝑜𝑈 =  . For convenient discussion, we set

0.5 as the threshold of IoU based on the intuition from computer
vision [53], with predictions deemed untrustworthy if 𝐼𝑜𝑈 ≤ 0.5..

This section presents the results of experiments evaluating Un-
trustVul in detecting untrustworthy predictions.
RQ1: Effectiveness
Intra-project setting. Table 2 shows the effectiveness of UntrustVul
and the baselines across four models under test on BigVul dataset.
UntrustVul consistently demonstrates strong effectiveness for
both transformer- and graph-based models, achieving Accuracy of
74%–84%, AUC of 77%–86%, F1-score of 82%–91%, and G-mean of
78%–80%. It outperforms Naive in all metrics, with improvements
of 16%–52% in Accuracy, 17%–54% in AUC, 13%–45% in F1-score,

and 22%–41% in G-mean. Compared to PerturbVar, PerturbAPI, and
PerturbJoint, UntrustVul improves by 29%–79% in Accuracy, 21%–
59% in AUC, 28%–90% in F1-score, and 27%–72% in G-mean. The
only exception is Specificity, where these baselines perform better,
indicating a bias towards labeling predictions as trustworthy. This
may be due to (1) spurious features beyond variable and API names,
or (2) inadequate perturbations failing to alter predictions.
Cross-project setting. Table 3 shows the effectiveness of UntrustVul
and the baselines in the cross-project setting onMegaVul and SARD
datasets, showing similar trends to the intra-project evaluation.
UntrustVul still consistently outperforms the baselines, with im-
provements of 4%–82% in Accuracy, 6%–59% in AUC, 1%–92% in
F1-score, and 13%–67% in G-mean. The baselines occasionally show
higher Precision and Specificity, reflecting a bias towards labeling
predictions as trustworthy. UntrustVul’s superior effectiveness
across datasets and models demonstrates its generalisability.
Impact of IoU thresholds. Figure 7 presents the average effective-
ness of UntrustVul and the baselines across four models and three
datasets at various IoU thresholds. PerturbJoint, the most effective
baseline among Rahman et al.’s approaches [43], is used alongside
Naive for comparison. Lower IoU thresholds relax ground-truth
trustworthiness labeling by allowing more irrelevant lines, result-
ing in more predictions labeled as trustworthy, while higher IoU
thresholds lead to more untrustworthy predictions.
With UntrustVul, all metrics increase as the IoU threshold in-
creases, showing better effectiveness under stricter trustworthiness
labeling. In contrast, the baselines exhibit more complex trends. For
Naive, AUC, Precision, and Specificity increase, while other metrics

and PerturbJoint based on their processing time (in seconds) for
individual functions. UntrustVul takes 1.02–1.47 seconds to assess
a prediction, depending on the size of the input function. Compared
to the baselines, UntrustVul requires an additional 0.51–0.90
seconds per function due to the use of Joern, the static code analysis
tool, and multiple line-level assessment models.
Answer to RQ2: UntrustVul requires only 1.02–1.47 seconds
to assess a prediction. Although it takes more time than the
baselines, the increase in time is insignificant considering given
its performance benefits over other approaches.
RQ3: Ablation Study
We compare UntrustVul with its ablation variants, including line-
level only, dependency-level only, and three versions that replace
ensemble learning with individual models. In the line-level only
variant, a prediction is trustworthy if most prediction-contributing
lines are classified as vulnerable candidates. In the dependency-
level only variant, vulnerable candidates are randomly selected. In
the variants without ensemble learning, UntrustVul uses single
models, CodeBERT [11], GraphCodeBERT [18], and UniXcoder [17].
Table 5 compares the effectiveness of UntrustVul and its abla-
tion variants. Overall, UntrustVul outperforms all variants.

The
line-level only and dependency-level only variants both signifi-
cantly decrease the effectiveness of UntrustVul by 11%–42% in
Accuracy, 3%–42% in AUC, 1%–37% in F1-score, and 4%–42% in
G-mean. While these variants occasionally achieve better in some
metrics, they consistently show lower G-mean, the balanced metric..

6.1  Implications
Detecting untrustworthy vulnerability predictions. Our study
supports findings of prior studies [4, 5, 14, 51], showing that SOTA
vulnerability detection models often make untrustworthy predic-
tions without fully reasoning about the code. Untrustworthy pre-
dictions can provide misleading suspicious lines, making it harder
for developers to identify actual vulnerabilities. Even worse, rely-
ing on these suspicious lines may result in flawed patches that fail
to fix existing vulnerabilities, or even introduce new ones. Hence,
despite the superior performance of these models, it is also im-
portant to assess their decision-making process. This paper intro-
duces UntrustVul, the first automated approach for detecting
untrustworthy vulnerability predictions. The promising results of
UntrustVul indicate that analysing the syntaxes of the suspicious
lines and their dependencies can be used to detect untrustworthy
behaviors of these models.
Automated trustworthiness assessment is an emerging yet essen-
tial challenge, not only for vulnerability detection but also for other
ML tasks, such as image classification and artifact generation. As
far as we know, this critical problem has received limited attention,
and we emphasize the need for further exploration.
Implications to SE. ML has been widely applied in SE to automate
many tasks in the SE lifecycle, such as planning, design, coding,
testing, and maintenance. However, organizations and individuals

increasingly question if they can trust ML and if they can demon-
strate trustworthiness [47]. The lack of trustworthiness poses a
significant barrier to the adoption of ML in SE. While making ML-
based systems fully trustworthy is the ultimate goal, achieving this
remains challenging. Hence, we believe a practical approach to
enhance their real-world applicability and usability is to provide
alerts when predictions are untrustworthy.
In addition, integrating UntrustVul into the SE lifecycle for
ML vulnerability detectors can greatly advance their development
and applications. Detectors relying on spurious correlations learnt
during training can achieve overconfident performance in held-
out evaluations. However, their performance often deteriorates in
the real world, where these correlations are absent [27, 37]. Un-
trustVul can serve as an automated oracle for trustworthiness
testing [56]. This testing process is crucial and closely intertwined
with other SE lifecycle activities [45], particularly in the iterative
development of ML-based systems, where performance and trust-
worthiness must be evaluated and refined [36]. By early detecting
trustworthiness issues during development, UntrustVul helps
prevent failures in real-world deployment. Furthermore, it offers
actionable insights that facilitate feedback-driven repair, as demon-
strated in Section 5, reducing the need for human intervention.
6.2  Threats to Validity
Threats to internal validity can be related to Joern, the static
source code analysis tool used to parse source code into PDGs.
Joern may fail to analyse some code snippets that contain complex
syntaxes. Hence, our experiments discard all the source code that
Joern is unable to parse. Another threat to internal validity is related
to code models used in line-level assessment, as they can be biased
due to their training data. We mitigate this by ensemble learning,
aggregating multiple SOTA pre-trained code models.
To reduce threats to external validity, we evaluate UntrustVul
on 155K predictions made by four SOTA vulnerability detection
models, including both transformer- and graph-basedmodels, across
three vulnerability datasets, including BigVul, MegaVul, and SARD.
Threats to construct validity can arise from the ground truths
used to evaluate UntrustVul. We leverage BigVul dataset to con-
struct the set of ground truths. Ground-truth trustworthiness labels
are determined bymeasuring Intersection over Union (IoU) between
lines in model explanations and lines in git commits addressing
vulnerabilities. However, several commits contain major changes,
with some modified lines unrelated to the vulnerabilities, such as
comments, line breaks, and refactored code. This label noise may
affect the experiments in Section 5. To address this threat, we apply
a lightweight approach using regex to filter out redundant lines.
7  RELATEDWORK
ML vulnerability detection models. They can be categorised
into token-based and graph-based models. Token-based models,
such as VulDeePecker [33] and SySeVR [32], treat source code as a
sequence of tokens, embedding them as vectors for training using
architectures like Bidirectional Long Short Term Memory and Con-
volutional Neural Networks. Recent models leverage transformers,
such as CodeBERT [11], GraphCodeBERT [18], and UniXcoder [17],
10

which are pre-trained in a self-supervised learning setting. For ex-
ample, LineVul [13] uses CodeBERT, while SVulD [40] combines
UniXcoder and contrastive learning, showcasing good performance.
In contrast, graph-based models represent the source code as
graphs that capture abstract syntax trees, control flow, and data
dependencies. By recognising relationships between lines of code,
these models are trained on both semantic and syntactic infor-
mation [7]. For instance, recent studies, such as ReVeal [4] and
IVDetect [31], leverage graph neural networks (GNNs) to build
vulnerability detection models and achieve accuracy of up to 90%.
Interpretability of ML vulnerability detection models. Due to the
lack of explainability, explanation methods has been introduced
to interpret predictions by identifying important input features.
Several methods [42] obtain importance scores for each feature
using gradients [49, 50] or the attention mechanism [19] to approx-
imate the feature importance. Other methods [30, 35, 61] perturb
the input and evaluate the model output changes. Recent meth-
ods [21, 44, 57, 63] locally approximate themodel as an interpretable
surrogate model. These explanation methods have been used in
vulnerability detection to locate vulnerabilities. For example, Line-
Vul [13] and mVulPreter [66] leverage the self-attentionmechanism
of transformers, while IVDetect [31] uses GNNExplainer [61].
Spurious correlations in ML vulnerability detection models. Nu-
merous studies [4, 5, 14, 51] have reported that these models often
rely on spurious correlations rather than root causes for predic-
tions.

These spurious correlations come from tokens frequently
appearing in training data, such as identifiers, keywords, and delim-
iters. To counteract spurious correlations, Gao et al. [15] proposed
a counterfactual reasoning-based framework to help capture the
misleading information from identifiers, and then mitigated it via
counterfactual inference. Imgrund et al. [23] presented three strate-
gies, including normalising the code by applying a consistent code
style, tokenising the code before feeding it into the model, and
causal learning. Rahman et al. [43] designed novel perturbations
to reveal overreliance on identifiers and applied casual learning to
mitigate such reliance.
Trustworthiness issues detection. Recent years have seen grow-
ing interest in the trustworthiness of ML across various areas [60],
such as computer vision [58], natural language processing [9, 56],
and healthcare [22]. However, only few methods support detecting
trustworthiness issues, most of which still rely on human valida-
tion [16, 27, 48]. Recently, Lam et al. [56] presented an automated
approach for generating trustworthiness oracles for text classifiers,
using explanations and word embeddings.
In vulnerability detection, Tien et al. [38] introduced HXAI-
VDIM, a framework that visualises explanations for human experts
to verify. Several studies [4, 5, 14, 51] manually examined expla-
nations to understand what vulnerability detection models learn.
Recent studies aim to automatically detect trustworthiness issues,
but they typically make specific assumptions, such as identifiers are
sources of these issues [15, 43]. In contrast, UntrustVul automati-
cally detects trustworthiness issues beyond identifiers by analysing
both syntax and dependencies, identifying predictions that do not
align with vulnerability patterns..

This paper proposes UntrustVul, the first automated approach for
detecting untrustworthy vulnerability predictions. Specifically, Un-
trustVul assesseswhether suspicious lines of code are vulnerability-
unrelated, in terms of syntactic meaning and dependencies, by ap-
plying supervised learning-based, static analysis, and rule-based
analysis techniques Experiments on four SOTA models and three
datasets show that UntrustVul outperforms the baselines and
ablation versions in detecting untrustworthy predictions and im-
proving vulnerability detectionmodels.While still in its early stages,
UntrustVul offers considerable opportunities for further explo-
ration. We plan to address the label noise problem to enhance
UntrustVul’s effectiveness. UntrustVul also has potential appli-
cations in other tasks, helping to build human trust in ML systems.
